narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 51.8% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold that leads to rejection. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles of 2 and 1 respectively, lower-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on what best signals bar exam success. The most influential element pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you low in your school compared to the much higher average (around the 6th decile) for those who passed the bar. This stood out as the strongest signal, suggesting challenges in sustaining strong performance later in your studies. Next in importance, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it was close to the average of 37.27 seen among successful bar takers. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 provided a positive boost, exceeding the 3.24 average for those who passed and helping offset some concerns. While your full-time enrollment aligns with the vast majority (over 93%) of passers and your family background fits within the broader distribution, they played smaller roles here. Overall, your profile shows strengths like a solid undergrad record, but the dip in later-year grades raised the biggest red flag when compared to typical accepted students, who maintain higher deciles throughout. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push closer to or above 37, strengthening your later-year performance through targeted coursework or tutoring, and reapplying with updated transcripts—these steps have helped many in similar situations turn predictions around. You're clearly capable, as your GPA demonstrates, and with focused improvements, a future application could look much stronger.
